interactionDescription: Metoclopramide may lower the absorption of Fosfomycin Tromethamine by speeding up digestion.
clinicalEffects: Lower blood and urine levels of fosfomycin.
managementAdvice: Avoid using together with Fosfomycin if possible. If needed, monitor for treatment effectiveness.

interactionDescription: Cimetidine does not affect Fosfomycin Tromethamine absorption or blood levels.
clinicalEffects: No effect on drug levels or effectiveness.

interactionDescription: No known interaction with beer; alcohol may affect general health while taking antibiotics.
clinicalEffects: None specifically reported for Fosfomycin Tromethamine.
managementAdvice: Consume alcohol moderately; avoid excessie drinking while ill or in antibiotics.

interactionDescription: No known interaction with wine; alcohol may affect general health while taking antibiotics.

interactionDescription: A high-fat meal may slow the absorption of Fosfomycin Tromethamine.
clinicalEffects: Lower peak blood level but same total amount excreted in urine.

interactionDescription: Metoclopramide may lower fosfomycin levels in the body by affecting how it is distributed.

interactionDescription: Using fosfomycin with gentamicin may increase stress on the kidneys.
managementAdvice: Monitor kidney function closely.

interactionDescription: Combining fosfomycin with vancomycin may increase the risk of kidney problems.

interactionDescription: Fosfomycin may lower potassium levels, and furosemide can also reduce potassium.
clinicalEffects: Increased risk of hypokalemia (weakness, irregular heartbeat).
managementAdvice: Monitor potassium levels regularly.

interactionDescription: Fosfomycin contains sodium, and additional sodium from IV fluids may increase total sodium load.
clinicalEffects: Fluid retention, high blood pressure, worsening heart failure.
managementAdvice: Monitor sodium levels and fluid status.

interactionDescription: Azithromycin has little or no effect on hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes, so may or may not interact with astemizole.

interactionDescription: Azithromycin has little or no effect on hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes, so may or may not interact with cisapride.

interactionDescription: Simultaneous administration of antacids had no effect on overall bioavailability but peak serum concentrations were reduced by approximately 24%.
managementAdvice: Do not take azithromycin and antacids simultaneously

interactionDescription: In elderly patients concurrently receiving diuretics, mainly thiazide, there appears to be an increased risk of thrombocytopenia with purpura.
managementAdvice: Use TMP-SMX with caution in elderly patients receiving thiazide diuretics

interactionDescription: Occasionally reports suggest that patients receiving pyrimethamine as malarial prophylaxis in doses exceeding 25 mg weekly may develop megaloblastic anaemia if Co- trimoxazole be prescribed concurrently.
managementAdvice: Avoid combination or closely monitor CBC for megaloblastic anemia.

interactionDescription: Cotrimoxazole has been shown to potentiate activity of strongly serum protein-bound drugs like oral hypoglycaemic or anticoagulant.
managementAdvice: Monitor for enhanced drug effects and adjust doses as needed.

interactionDescription: It has been reported that comazole may displace the anticoagulant warfarin. Careful control of the anticoagulant therapy during treatment with Comazole is advisable.
managementAdvice: Monitor INR closely and reduce warfarin dose if necessary.

interactionDescription: Comazole also may inhibit the hepatic metabolism of phenytoin. A 39% increase in phenytoin half-life and 27% decrease in the metabolic clearance rate of phenytoin have been observed following administration of Comazole of normal clinical dosages. If these two drugs are given concurrently it is important to remain alert for signs of phenytoin toxicity.
managementAdvice: Monitor serum phenytoin levels and signs of toxicity. Dose adjustment may be required.

interactionDescription: Concurrent use of rifampicin and Comazole results in shortening of plasma half life of trimethoprim.
managementAdvice: Monitor clinical response as trimethoprim levels may be reduced. Consider alternative therapy.

interactionDescription: Concurrent use of warfarin and azithromycin may potentiate the effects of oral anticoagulants.
clinicalEffects: None specifically reported in the dedicated drug interaction study.
managementAdvice: Prothrombin time should be carefully monitored while patients are receiving azithromycin and oral anticoagulants concurrently

interactionDescription: Co-administration with a single oral dose of azithromycin resulted in increased azithromycin serum concetration.
clinicalEffects: Dose adjustment of azithromycin is not recommended when administered in combination with nelfinavir.
managementAdvice: Close monitoring for known adverse effects of azithromycin, such as liver enzyme abnormalities and hearing impairment.

clinicalEffects: Reduced clearance by inhibiting renal tubular secretion

clinicalEffects: Slows renal secretion, producing higher and prolonged serum levels

interactionDescription: Concomitant use with penicillins
clinicalEffects: May cause breakthrough bleeding and reduce birth control effectiveness

interactionDescription: Concurrent use with aminopenicillins
clinicalEffects: Inhibits renal tubular secretion, increasing drug levels
managementAdvice: Carefully monitor clinical status during co-administration

interactionDescription: Taken simultaneously or shortly before
clinicalEffects: Produces higher and prolonged serum concentrations
managementAdvice: Physician supervision recommended

clinicalEffects: Increases incidence of skin rashes, especially in hyperuricemic patients
managementAdvice: Monitor for allergic manifestations

interactionDescription: Taking medication during meals
clinicalEffects: Absorption is 90% and is not significantly affected by food
managementAdvice: May be taken with meals for better absorption and less Gastrointestinal discomfort

interactionDescription: Sulfonamides such as sulfamethoxazole may displace methotrexate from protein binding sites and can compete with the renal transport of methotrexate, thereby increasing free methotrexate levels. Cases of pancytopenia have been reported in patients taking the combination of sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim and methotrexate.
managementAdvice: Avoid combination. Monitor for increased free methotrexate and marrow suppression.

interactionDescription: Para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) or its derivatives may antagonise the antibacterial effects of sulfamethoxazole.
managementAdvice: Avoid PABA supplements. They may reduce TMP‑SMX efficacy.

interactionDescription: Anticoagulant activity may be increased by concurrent treatment with sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim. It has been reported that sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim may prolong the prothrombin time in patients who are receiving the anticoagulant warfarin (a CYP2C9 substrate). This interaction should be kept in mind when sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim is given to patients already on anticoagulant therapy, and the coagulation time should be reassessed.
managementAdvice: Monitor INR/PT. Adjust dose to avoid over-anticoagulation.

interactionDescription: Increased effects and side effects of phenytoin (folate deficiencies) could occur when sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim is given concurrently. Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim may inhibit the hepatic metabolism of phenytoin (a CYP2C9 substrate). Oral sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim, given at a common clinical dosage, increased the phenytoin half-life by 39% and decreased the phenytoin metabolic clearance rate by 27%. When administering these drugs concurrently, one should be alert for possible excessive phenytoin effect.
managementAdvice: Monitor levels and for toxicity. Adjust dose as needed.

interactionDescription: Concomitant use may result in potentiation of hypoglycaemia in occasional patients.
managementAdvice: Monitor blood glucose for potentiated hypoglycemia.

interactionDescription: An increased incidence of thrombocytopenia is reported when this combination is used in the elderly.
managementAdvice: Monitor for thrombocytopenia. Avoid if high risk.

interactionDescription:
Deterioration in renal function in patients with renal transplants. There have been reports of marked but reversible nephrotoxicity with co-
administration of sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim and ciclosporin in renal transplant recipients.
managementAdvice: Avoid combination if possible. Monitor renal function.

interactionDescription: Occasional reports suggest that patients receiving pyrimethamine as malarial prophylaxis at doses in excess of 25 mg weekly may develop megaloblastic anaemia should sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim be prescribed concurrently.
managementAdvice: Avoid combination. Monitor CBC for anemia.

interactionDescription: Concomitant use of trimethoprim with digoxin has been shown to increase plasma digoxin levels in a proportion of elderly patients. Serum digoxin levels should be monitored.
clinicalEffects: Digoxin toxicity which includes nausea, arrhythmias
managementAdvice: Monitor serum digoxin. Adjust dose to avoid toxicity.

interactionDescription: Due to the potassium sparing effects of sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim, caution should be used when other agents that increase serum potassium, such as angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, potassium sparing diuretics and prednisolone, are co-administered. In the literature, two cases of hyperkalaemia in elderly patients have been reported after concomitant intake of sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim and an angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitor.
managementAdvice: Monitor serum potassium. Avoid in high-risk patients.

interactionDescription: The efficacy of tricyclic antidepressants can decrease when co-administered with sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim.
managementAdvice: Monitor therapeutic response. Adjust TCA dose if efficacy decreases.

interactionDescription: May occur in patients who are also receiving urinary acidifiers, oral anticoagulants, phenylbutazone, oxyphenbutazone and indomethacin.
clinicalEffects: Increased TMP-SMX blood levels, risk of adverse effects
managementAdvice: Avoid combination. Monitor for toxicity.

interactionDescription: Trimethoprim is an inhibitor of CYP2C8 as well as an OCT2 transporter. Sulfamethoxazole is an inhibitor of CYP2C9. Caution is recommended when DBL Sulfamethoxazole 400 mg and Trimethoprim 80 mg is co-administered with drugs that are substrates of CYP2C8 and 2C9 or OCT2. Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim potentiates the effect of oral hypoglycaemics that are metabolised by CYP2C8 (e.g. pioglitazone, repaglinide, and rosiglitazone) or CYP2C9 (e.g. glipizide and glyburide) or eliminated renally via OCT2 (e.g. metformin). Additional monitoring of blood glucose may be warranted.
clinicalEffects: Increased drug levels or potentiation, hypoglycemia, toxicity
managementAdvice: Monitor for altered effects. Adjust therapy based on response.

interactionDescription: Concurrent administration is contraindicated. Elevated plasma concentrations of dofetilide have been reported following concurrent administration of trimethoprim and dofetilide. Increased plasma concentrations of dofetilide may cause serious ventricular arrhythmias associated with QT interval prolongation, including torsade de pointes.
clinicalEffects: Elevated dofetilide levels is linear with QT prolongation, torsade de pointes, serious ventricular arrhythmias
managementAdvice: Contraindicated with TMP‑SMX. Avoid co-administration

interactionDescription: When trimethoprim is administered simultaneously with drugs that form cations at physiological pH, and are also partly excreted by active renal secretion (e.g. procainamide, amantadine), there is the possibility of competitive inhibition of this process which may lead to an increase in plasma concentration of one or both of the drugs.
clinicalEffects: Increased plasma levels, risk of toxicity
managementAdvice: Monitor drug levels. Watch for increased plasma concentrations.

interactionDescription: This drug reduces the kidney's ability to remove nitrofurantoin from the body, which can make the antibiotic stay longer in the bloodstream.
clinicalEffects: Higher levels of nitrofurantoin in the body can increase the risk of side effects such as nausea, liver problems, nerve issues, and general toxicity.
managementAdvice: Avoid taking probenecid and nitrofurantoin together. If combination is necessary, monitor closely for side effects and adjust dosage under medical supervision.

interactionDescription: Antacids that contain magnesium can bind with nitrofurantoin in the stomach or intestines and reduce how much of the drugs is absorbed into the blood.
clinicalEffects: Reduced absorption can make the antibiotic less effective at treating urinary tract infections, potentially allowing the bacteria to persist or worsen.
managementAdvice: Do not take nitrofurantoin at the same time as magnesium-containing antacids. Take the antibiotic at least 2 hours before or after antacids to ensure proper absorption.

interactionDescription: When taken together, fluoroquinolone antibiotics may have an antagonistic effect on nitrofurantoin, meaning the two drugs can interfere with each other's ability to kill bacteria.
clinicalEffects: Reduced antibacterial effectiveness can lead to slower infections recovery, persistent infection, or increased risk of bacterial resistance.
managementAdvice: Avoid combining nitrofurantoin with fluoroquinolone antibiotics unless directed by a doctor. If both are needed, careful monitoring and alternative dosing strategies may be required.

interactionDescription: Antacids that contain magnesium trisilicate can stick to Macrobid in your stomach and gut, which lowers the amount of the antibiotic that your body can absorb. This makes Macrobid less able to fight the bacteria causing your infection.
clinicalEffects: Because less Macrobid reaches your bloodstream and urine, the infection may not get treated properly. You may have ongoing symptoms of urinary tract infection like pain, burning, or frequent urination.
managementAdvice: Avoid taking these antacids while on Macrobid. If you must take an antacid, separate the doses by at least 2-3 hours. Always tell your doctor or pharmacist about any antacids you are using.

interactionDescription: Drugs like probenecid or sulfinpyrazone (used for gout) can block the way your kidneys remove Macrobid from your blood. This can cause higher levels of the drug in your body but lower levels in your urine where it is needed to fight bacteria.
clinicalEffects: Higher blood levels can increase your risk of side effects like nausea, headache, or nerve problems. Lower urine levels may make Macrobid less effective, leading to persistent urinary infections.
managementAdvice: Your doctor may monitor your blood and urine closely if you take these drugs with Macrobid. They may adjust your dose or suggest using a different medication combination to avoid problems.

interactionDescription: Antacids containing magnesium trisilicate can bind to Furadantin in the stomach and intestines, reducing absorption of the antibiotic into the bloodstream.
clinicalEffects: Lower levels of Furadantin in the urine may make the drug less effective at treating urinary tract infections, which could lead to persistent infection.
managementAdvice: Avoid taking magnesium trisilicate antacids while on Furadantin. If necessary, separate doses by several hours to ensure proper absorption. Always inform your doctor or pharmacist.

interactionDescription: Probenecid, a uricosuric drug, can reduce kidney excretion of Furadantin, increasing drug levels in the blood.
clinicalEffects: Higher blood levels may increase risk of side effects such as nausea, dizziness, or neuropathy, while lower drug concentration in urine can reduce effectiveness against urinary infections.
managementAdvice: Monitor for side effects. Your doctor may adjust doses of Furadantin or the uricosuric drug. Report any persistent urinary symptoms.

interactionDescription: Sulfinpyrazone, another uricosuric agent, can similarly interfere with kidney excretion of Furadantin.
clinicalEffects: Blood levels of Furadantin may rise, increasing side effect risks, while effectiveness in treating urinary tract infections may decrease.
managementAdvice: Monitor for side effects and infection signs. Your doctor may need to adjust treatment. Avoid combining without supervision.

interactionDescription: Propantheline, an anticholinergic drug, can alter kidney excretion of Furadantin, slightly changing how much antibiotic reaches the urine.
clinicalEffects: This may reduce the effectiveness of Furadantin in treating urinary infections, though exact clinical impact is uncertain.
managementAdvice: Your doctor may monitor infection progress. Report any persistent symptoms and continue therapy exactly as prescribed.

interactionDescription: Probenecid reduces the kidney's ability to remove Nitrofurantoin from the body through urine. This causes the drug to stay longer in the bloodstream instead of being properly excreted into the urine where it is needed to treat infection.
clinicalEffects: Higher levels of Nitrofurantoin may build up in the blood, increasing the risk of side effects. At the same time, lower levels in the urine may reduce its ability to effectively kill bacteria in urinary tract infections.
managementAdvice: Avoid using together unless prescribed by a doctor. The healthcare provider may monitor closely for side effects or adjust the treatment if necessary.

interactionDescription: Sulfinpyrazone also decreases the excretion of Nitrofurantoin by the kidneys. This changes the normal balance of the drug in the body.
clinicalEffects: Increased blood levels may raise the chance of side effects, while decreased urinary levels may reduce the drug's effectiveness in treating urinary infections.
managementAdvice: Use with caution and only under medical supervision. Report any unusual side effects or persistent infection symptoms.

interactionDescription: Magnesium Trisilicate reduces the absorption of Nitrofurantoin from the stomach and intestines. This means less of the antibiotic enters the bloodstream.
clinicalEffects: Lower absorption can reduce the amount of medicine reaching the urine. making treatment less effective. The infection may not fully clear.
managementAdvice: Avoid taking magnesium-containing antacids at the same time. If necessary, separate doses by several hours. Consult a healthcare provider before combining.

interactionDescription: Quinolone antibacterials may have antagonistic effects when used together with Nitrofurantoin. This means the medicines may reduce each other's effectiveness.
clinicalEffects: The combined use may decrease the overall antibacterial action, leading to reduced treatment effectiveness and possible treatment failure.
managementAdvice: Avoid combining these antibiotics unless specifically directed by a physician. Follow medical advice carefully.

interactionDescription: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors reduce the effectiveness of Nitrofurantoin by making the urine less acidic. Nitrofurantoin works better in acidic urine.
clinicalEffects: Reduced antibacterial activity may occur, leading to incomplete treatment of urinary tract infections.
managementAdvice: Use cautiously. The doctor may monitor treatment response and adjust therapy if needed.

interactionDescription: Urinary alkalinisers increase urine pH (make urine more alkaline), which can decrease the activity of Nitrofurantoin.
clinicalEffects: The medicine may not work as effectively, and the infection may persist or return.
managementAdvice: Avoid unnecessary use of urinary alkalinisers while taking Nitrofurantoin. Inform your doctor about all medications being used.
